issues.body: Both `for lb in ${{ join(github.event.issue.labels.*.name, ' ') }}` loops splice label names into the generated shell source without quoting or structured transport. GitHub label names may contain shell metacharacters; for example, a crafted label containing command substitution becomes executable shell when an admin later applies `set-up-account`. The actor/author permission check does not make every pre-existing label name safe: another collaborator can add a label before the trusted actor triggers the run. The injected command receives the workflow `GITHUB_TOKEN`, whose declared `contents: write` permission can mutate this trust-plane repository. This also violates the workflow's own stated structured-data handling approach.
issues.fix_hint: Pass the labels through a structured channel (for example `toJSON(...)` in an environment variable) and parse the JSON in Python or jq; never interpolate label text into `run:` source. Validate targets with an exact repository-name grammar and add a regression/self-test covering spaces, quotes, `$()`, semicolons, and newlines in unrelated and target-prefixed label names. Carry derived targets between steps via a structured or safely encoded representation rather than a space-delimited shell string.
issues.title: Issue label names are interpolated directly into a privileged shell script

summary: The scoped grant and live-label equality checks remain sound, but the newly added target-label capture interpolates the issue's complete label list directly into a privileged shell script. A crafted pre-existing label can execute shell syntax when an administrator later applies set-up-account. Because this workflow has contents:write, that is a trust-plane command-injection path and must block approval. Round 3 recorded no issues, so this newly identified blocker makes progress regressing.
